page: Alibaba Cloud ecs.hfr6.16xlarge is a memory-optimized virtual machine instance powered by the KVM hypervisor. It features an x86_64 architecture with 64 dedicated physical cores and 64 vCPUs, utilizing the Intel Xeon 8269CY processor running at a clock speed of 3.1 GHz. With a 1.0 thread per core configuration, each vCPU maps to a dedicated physical core, ensuring consistent compute performance without resource sharing.

The instance is equipped with 512.0 GB of system memory, providing a high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 8.0 GB per core. This dense memory configuration is designed to support memory-intensive workloads. The instance does not include local NVMe or SSD storage, meaning all storage requirements must be met via network-attached block storage. Network connectivity is supported by a baseline bandwidth of 20 Gbps.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, the dedicated CPU allocation and high memory density offer a balanced resource profile for enterprise workloads that require predictable compute performance and large memory footprints without the overhead of local storage. This makes the instance suitable for large-scale databases, in-memory caching layers, enterprise resource planning systems, and memory-bound data processing applications.
description: Alibaba Cloud ecs.hfr6.16xlarge is a memory-optimized instance built on the KVM hypervisor and x86_64 architecture. It features 64 dedicated physical cores and 64 vCPUs powered by an Intel Xeon 8269CY processor operating at 3.1 GHz. With a 1.0 thread per core configuration, it delivers dedicated compute resources. The instance is equipped with 512.0 GB of RAM, yielding a high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 8.0 GB per core. It does not include local storage, relying instead on network-attached storage options, and provides a baseline network bandwidth of 20 Gbps. This resource profile offers qualitative cost efficiency for memory-heavy workloads by providing dedicated CPU performance and high memory density. It is well-suited for in-memory databases, large-scale caching, and enterprise applications.
